AI.tldr: An uncertainty-aware Agentic RAG framework that uses Bayesian Networks to propagate uncertainty signals across multi-stage reasoning pipelines for failure point detection and system reliability estimation.
AI.motivation: Trustworthy deployment of Agentic RAG systems requires mechanisms for estimating when multi-stage reasoning pipelines may fail, necessitating uncertainty quantification and failure point identification.
AI.method: Developed an uncertainty-aware Agentic RAG framework where planner, evaluator, and generator stages produce uncertainty signals from semantic divergence and self-evaluation, propagated through a Bayesian Network to estimate system-level uncertainty and identify node-level failure points.
AI.result: Bayesian propagation is more effective on HotpotQA (where uncertainty accumulates across multi-hop reasoning) than StrategyQA (which exposed limitations from miscalibration and unreliable upstream signals). Evaluation used AUROC, AUARC, ECE, and Brier Score on StrategyQA and HotpotQA with GPT-3.5-Turbo and GPT-4.1-Nano.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian uncertainty propagation is a promising but preliminary mechanism for monitoring Agentic RAG systems, requiring future validation in industrial domains like Offshore Wind maintenance decision support.

title: K-Inverse-RFM: A Modified RFM that Bridges the Gap to Neural Networks for Data-Corrupted Mathematical Tasks
comment: Master's thesis, University of California San Diego, 2025
summary: Recursive Feature Machines (RFMs) are a class of kernel machines that utilize the Average Gradient Outer Product (AGOP) as a mechanism for feature learning. They have been shown to effectively replicate the learning dynamics and feature representations of Feedforward Neural Networks (FNNs) across various settings. However, despite comparable capacity for feature learning and the similarities in the features they acquire, RFMs exhibit significantly lower performance than neural networks in certain data-corrupted scenarios. In this work, we investigate these limitations in mathematical problems. As a solution, we introduce a remarkably effective transformation applied to the training labels which promotes learning in noisy, complexly represented, and class-imbalanced data. This simple yet powerful adjustment enables RFMs to close the performance gap with FNNs and, in some cases, even surpass them.

AI.tldr: RFMs are kernel machines that can learn features like neural networks but fail in noisy/imbalanced data; a simple label transformation fixes this gap.
AI.motivation: RFMs effectively replicate neural network feature learning but show significantly lower performance in data-corrupted scenarios like noisy, complex, or imbalanced data. The paper aims to investigate these limitations and find a solution to close the performance gap.
AI.method: The authors introduce a simple but effective transformation applied to training labels that promotes learning in challenging data scenarios. This adjustment enables RFMs to better handle noisy, complexly represented, and class-imbalanced data.
AI.result: The label transformation enables RFMs to close the performance gap with Feedforward Neural Networks and, in some cases, even surpass them. The solution is remarkably effective despite its simplicity.
AI.conclusion: A simple label transformation can overcome RFMs' limitations in challenging data scenarios, allowing them to match or exceed neural network performance, demonstrating that RFMs' underperformance in corrupted data is addressable through appropriate training modifications.

title: PedNStream: Scalable Network Flow Simulation for Pedestrian Traffic Management
summary: Large-scale crowd management requires pedestrian simulations that are both computationally efficient and compatible with feedback-based control. However, most open-source tools are either microscopic or not designed for network-scale closed-loop evaluation. This paper presents PedNStream (Pedestrian Network Flow Simulation), an open-source, Python-native simulator for macroscopic pedestrian network loading based on the Link Transmission Model (LTM). The framework extends LTM-based pedestrian models by incorporating stochastic link dynamics that capture diffusion and activity-induced variability, and replaces dynamic user equilibrium route choice with a utility-based formulation suited to uncertain, intervention-driven settings. PedNStream is implemented as a modular framework with built-in controller interfaces for interventions such as gating, flow separation, and route guidance. We evaluate the framework in a staged manner. Synthetic scenarios verify key mechanisms, including queue formation, spillback, congestion dissipation, and adaptive rerouting. Real-network experiments assess large-scale behavior and consistency with observed pedestrian counts. A closed-loop case study demonstrates controller integration, and a runtime analysis quantifies scalability. These results establish PedNStream as an efficient and practical testbed for large-scale pedestrian network simulation and control.

AI.tldr: PedNStream: An open-source Python simulator for macroscopic pedestrian network flow simulation with control interfaces for crowd management interventions.
AI.motivation: Large-scale crowd management requires pedestrian simulations that are both computationally efficient and compatible with feedback-based control. Existing open-source tools are either microscopic or not designed for network-scale closed-loop evaluation.
AI.method: Presents PedNStream, an open-source Python-native simulator based on the Link Transmission Model (LTM). Extends LTM with stochastic link dynamics capturing diffusion and activity variability, and replaces dynamic user equilibrium with utility-based route choice for uncertain, intervention-driven settings. Implemented as modular framework with built-in controller interfaces for interventions like gating, flow separation, and route guidance.
AI.result: Evaluated in staged manner: synthetic scenarios verify key mechanisms (queue formation, spillback, congestion dissipation, adaptive rerouting); real-network experiments assess large-scale behavior and consistency with observed counts; closed-loop case study demonstrates controller integration; runtime analysis quantifies scalability. Shows computational efficiency and practical utility.
AI.conclusion: PedNStream establishes itself as an efficient and practical testbed for large-scale pedestrian network simulation and control, addressing the gap for network-scale, closed-loop evaluation tools in crowd management.

title: PRISM: Prioritized Channel Importance with Semi-supervised Domain Adaptation for Cross-Subject EEG Emotion Recognition
summary: Electroencephalogram (EEG) captures endogenous brain activity with high temporal fidelity and holds substantial promise for precise emotion decoding. However, channel redundancy and pronounced inter-subject variability remain key obstacles to scalable generalization. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework termed PRioritized channel Importance with Semi-supervised doMain adaptation (PRISM), enabling label-efficient cross-subject emotion decoding. On the channel side, PRISM assigns differentiable, data-dependent channel weights via a lightweight expert ensemble, amplifying reliable electrodes while suppressing distractors. On the domain side, PRISM leverages unlabeled data through confidence-filtered pseudo-labels to drive consistency regularization and domain alignment, mitigating subject-specific heterogeneity. Extensive experiments show that PRISM surpasses state-of-the-art methods on DEAP, DREAMER, and SEED datasets, achieving robust cross-subject generalization given limited annotations.

AI.tldr: PRISM is a novel EEG-based emotion decoding framework that addresses channel redundancy and inter-subject variability through prioritized channel weighting and semi-supervised domain adaptation for label-efficient cross-subject generalization.
AI.motivation: EEG offers high temporal resolution for emotion decoding but faces challenges with channel redundancy (irrelevant electrodes) and significant inter-subject variability, which hinder scalable generalization across individuals.
AI.method: PRISM uses a lightweight expert ensemble to assign differentiable, data-dependent channel weights to prioritize reliable electrodes while suppressing distractors. It also employs semi-supervised domain adaptation with confidence-filtered pseudo-labels for consistency regularization and domain alignment to mitigate subject-specific heterogeneity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on DEAP, DREAMER, and SEED datasets demonstrate that PRISM surpasses state-of-the-art methods and achieves robust cross-subject generalization with limited annotation requirements.
AI.conclusion: PRISM effectively addresses key challenges in EEG-based emotion decoding by combining channel importance prioritization with semi-supervised domain adaptation, enabling label-efficient and generalizable emotion recognition across subjects.

title: Agentic generation of verifiable rules for deterministic, self-expanding reaction classification
summary: Computer-assisted synthesis planning breaks target molecules into accessible precursors using large libraries of reaction rules that assign each transformation a deterministic, interpretable label. But chemistry is long-tailed, making manual encoding intractable, and existing tools rely on fixed rulesets that cannot adapt to new chemistries. Here we present a fully automated pipeline in which a multi-agent framework of large language models (LLMs) classifies reactions and writes the rules themselves across 665,901 US patent reactions, generating each rule under a verification loop that tests it against the corpus. It expands a standard taxonomy from 68 to 14,073 classes without human curation. With a lightweight fingerprint classifier, it classifies 97.7\% of unseen reactions, matching a leading proprietary classifier while resolving chemistry more finely and extending on demand to chemistry outside its training distribution. The result is a living reactivity database and a general route to turning generative models into reliable, self-expanding symbolic systems.

AI.tldr: An AI system uses large language models to automatically generate and verify chemical reaction rules from patents, expanding from 68 to 14,073 reaction classes without human intervention, achieving 97.7% classification accuracy on unseen reactions.
AI.motivation: Current computer-assisted synthesis planning relies on fixed, manually-encoded reaction rule libraries that cannot adapt to new chemistries or cover chemistry's long-tailed nature, making manual encoding intractable.
AI.method: A multi-agent framework of large language models classifies reactions and writes reaction rules automatically across 665,901 US patent reactions. Each rule is generated under a verification loop that tests it against the corpus.
AI.result: The system expands a standard taxonomy from 68 to 14,073 reaction classes without human curation. With a lightweight fingerprint classifier, it achieves 97.7% classification accuracy on unseen reactions, matching proprietary classifiers while resolving chemistry more finely and extending to chemistry outside its training distribution.
AI.conclusion: The approach creates a living reactivity database and provides a general route to turn generative AI models into reliable, self-expanding symbolic systems that can automatically adapt to new chemical knowledge.

title: SAOT: Self-Supervised Continual Graph Learning with Structure-Aware Optimal Transport
comment: The paper has 9 pages of text and 13 pages in total (including acknowledgments, impact statement, references, and appendix), with 6 figures and 4 tables. This paper has been accepted by ICML 2026 conference and this is a final version of the manuscript submitted to the conference
summary: Self-supervised Continual Graph Learning (CGL) aims to successively learn from a graph sequence with different tasks without label supervision - a paradigm that has attracted widespread attention. Most existing self-supervised CGL methods rely on instance-level consistency objectives that enforce stability of individual node (or node-pair) embeddings. Due to optimizing nodes in isolation, these methods fail to maintain global relational structure, causing inter-node correspondences to progressively distort under continual learning. To this end, we propose a novel Structure-Aware Optimal Transport (SAOT) framework that explicitly captures and preserves relational structure within graph representations across sequential tasks. Specifically, SAOT leverages optimal transport theory to capture global inter-node correspondences, thereby facilitating and enhancing graph representation learning. Simultaneously, SAOT incorporates a cross-task knowledge distillation mechanism to preserve the previous structural knowledge. Extensive experiments on four CGL benchmark datasets demonstrate that SAOT outperforms existing self-supervised baselines. In particular, SAOT achieves significant performance gains, improving average accuracy by up to 5% on CoraFull-CL and over 15% on Products-CL compared with state-of-the-art methods in the Class-IL setting.

AI.tldr: SAOT: A Structure-Aware Optimal Transport framework for self-supervised continual graph learning that maintains global relational structure across sequential tasks using optimal transport theory and cross-task knowledge distillation.
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised continual graph learning methods focus on instance-level consistency (individual node embeddings), which fails to maintain global relational structure. This causes progressive distortion of inter-node correspondences under continual learning.
AI.method: Proposes Structure-Aware Optimal Transport (SAOT) framework that: 1) Uses optimal transport theory to capture global inter-node correspondences for enhanced graph representation learning, and 2) Incorporates cross-task knowledge distillation to preserve previous structural knowledge.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four CGL benchmark datasets show SAOT outperforms existing self-supervised baselines. Achieves up to 5% average accuracy improvement on CoraFull-CL and over 15% on Products-CL compared to state-of-the-art methods in Class-IL setting.
AI.conclusion: SAOT effectively addresses the limitation of existing methods by explicitly preserving relational structure through optimal transport theory and knowledge distillation, achieving superior performance in self-supervised continual graph learning.

title: Can Agents Generalize to the Open World? Unveiling the Fragility of Static Training in Tool Use
summary: While Large Language Model (LLM) agents demonstrate proficiency in static benchmarks, their deployment in real-world scenarios is hindered by the dynamic nature of user queries, tool sets, and interaction dynamics. To address this generalization gap, we formalize OpenAgent (Tool-Use Agent in Open-World), a problem setting characterized by distributional shifts across query, action, observation, and domain dimensions. To systematically diagnose its impact, we construct a controlled sandbox environment where we define fine-grained environmental shifts across a four-tier hierarchy, Perception, Interaction, Reasoning, and Internalization, and conduct a comprehensive series of experiments. Our analysis yields a series of key insights, demonstrating that agents trained via both Supervised Fine-Tuning(SFT) and Reinforcement Learning suffer from varying degrees of performance degradation when confronting open environmental shifts. Building on these insights, we propose Perturbation-Augmented Fine-Tuning, a disturbance-based intervention strategy for SFT that lays the foundation for enhancing agent robustness and utility in realistic environments. Our code will be released at: https://github. com/LAMDA-NeSy/OpenAgent.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the generalization gap of LLM agents in real-world scenarios by formalizing the OpenAgent problem, analyzing performance degradation under environmental shifts, and proposing a perturbation-augmented fine-tuning method to improve agent robustness.
AI.motivation: LLM agents perform well on static benchmarks but struggle in real-world deployment due to dynamic user queries, changing tool sets, and evolving interaction dynamics. The motivation is to bridge this generalization gap and address the challenges of open-world scenarios with distributional shifts.
AI.method: The authors formalize the OpenAgent problem setting with distributional shifts across query, action, observation, and domain dimensions. They construct a controlled sandbox environment with a four-tier hierarchy (Perception, Interaction, Reasoning, Internalization) to systematically diagnose the impact of environmental shifts. They analyze both SFT and RL-trained agents and propose Perturbation-Augmented Fine-Tuning, a disturbance-based intervention strategy for SFT.
AI.result: Analysis reveals that both SFT and RL-trained agents suffer from varying degrees of performance degradation when facing open environmental shifts. The paper provides key insights into how different types of shifts affect agent performance across the four-tier hierarchy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Perturbation-Augmented Fine-Tuning lays a foundation for enhancing agent robustness and utility in realistic environments. The OpenAgent framework provides a systematic way to study and address the generalization challenges of LLM agents in open-world scenarios.

title: Learning Generalizable Skill Policy with Data-Efficient Unsupervised RL
summary: Unsupervised Reinforcement Learning (URL) aims to pre-train scalable, skill-conditioned policies without extrinsic rewards, serving as a foundation for downstream control tasks. Despite recent progress, we argue that current off-policy URL methods are limited by two critical, overlooked bottlenecks: (1) non-stationary skill semantics and (2) brittle generalization. To address these challenges, we propose GenDa (Generalizable Data-efficient Agent), a unified framework for robust unsupervised reinforcement learning. First, we introduce a skill relabeling mechanism to mitigate non-stationarity and significantly improve data efficiency for pre-training. Second, we propose a Complementary Information Bottleneck (CIB), encouraging the learned skill policy to focus on ego-centric features and become robust to distribution shifts for downstream tasks. Through various experiments, we demonstrate that GenDa significantly enhances the scalability of URL with superior generalizability and data efficiency. Our code and videos are available at https://ihatebroccoli.github.io/official-GenDa.

AI.tldr: GenDa is a unified framework for unsupervised reinforcement learning that addresses non-stationary skill semantics and brittle generalization through skill relabeling and Complementary Information Bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Current off-policy unsupervised RL methods have two critical bottlenecks: (1) non-stationary skill semantics where skill representations change during training, and (2) brittle generalization where learned skills don't transfer well to downstream tasks with distribution shifts.
AI.method: GenDa proposes two key components: 1) Skill relabeling mechanism to mitigate non-stationarity and improve data efficiency, and 2) Complementary Information Bottleneck (CIB) that encourages skill policies to focus on ego-centric features for robustness to distribution shifts.
AI.result: Experiments show GenDa significantly enhances the scalability of unsupervised RL with superior generalizability and data efficiency compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: GenDa successfully addresses the key limitations of current unsupervised RL methods, providing a robust framework that improves skill learning through better stability and generalization capabilities.

title: Optimal Resource Utilization for Autonomous Laboratory Orchestrators
summary: In autonomous laboratories, AI agents suggest the next batch of experiments to do. However, planning and executing those tasks taking full advantage of the available resources is a completely different question. This can be challenging when dealing with real-world hardware constraints, especially so when there are multiple instruments with different capacities and throughputs. Here we demonstrate a 2-step method to address resource utilization for our autonomous platform for metal-organic framework synthesis. First, we use constraint programming to find optimal schedules. This finds schedules that minimizes the total time while still satisfying the limitations and capacities of the hardware. Secondly, we use a system of status dependencies for each task, which allows for the robust execution of the optimal schedules.

AI.tldr: A 2-step method combining constraint programming for optimal scheduling and status dependency systems for robust execution in autonomous MOF synthesis laboratories.
AI.motivation: Planning and executing experiments efficiently in autonomous laboratories is challenging due to real-world hardware constraints, especially with multiple instruments having different capacities and throughputs.
AI.method: Two-step approach: 1) Uses constraint programming to find optimal schedules that minimize total time while satisfying hardware limitations, and 2) Implements a system of status dependencies for each task to enable robust execution of optimal schedules.
AI.result: Demonstrated method successfully addresses resource utilization challenges for an autonomous platform dedicated to metal-organic framework (MOF) synthesis.
AI.conclusion: The 2-step approach effectively solves the resource optimization problem in autonomous laboratories by combining mathematical scheduling optimization with robust execution mechanisms.

title: Timesynth: A Temporal Fidelity Framework for Health Signal Digital Twins
summary: Forecasting models for health-signal digital twins must preserve the oscillatory, frequency, phase, and state-transition dynamics of physiological signals, yet the pointwise metrics used to benchmark them cannot detect when these fundamental properties are lost. We show that this blind spot misranks models: across 11 architectures, models with comparable pointwise error diverge by up to 53° in phase accuracy, equivalent to roughly 123 ms for a 1.2 Hz cardiac rhythm and invisible to standard metrics. To enable development of models that escape such failures, we introduce TimeSynth, a controlled benchmarking framework with two reusable components: a physiologically grounded generator producing signals with analytically known ground-truth dynamics from parametric models fitted to real electroencephalography, electrocardiography and photoplethysmogram signals, along with diagnostics quantifying amplitude, frequency, phase, and state-transition fidelity. Linear and full-sequence attention models systematically lose frequency and phase information despite acceptable amplitude error, whereas architectures with localized temporal structure better preserve dynamical fidelity and adapt to observable state transitions; none, however, reliably preserves stochastic switching. Because the dominant determinant of fidelity is architectural, model choice becomes a principled, use-case-driven decision rather than a search for a single winner. TimeSynth thus supplies the controlled preclinical stress test missing before models are coupled to patient data, with a reusable generator and diagnostics for fidelity-aware development.

AI.tldr: TimeSynth is a benchmarking framework that reveals how standard pointwise metrics fail to detect when forecasting models lose key physiological signal dynamics (phase, frequency, oscillatory properties), showing architectural choices determine fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current forecasting models for health-signal digital twins use pointwise error metrics that cannot detect when fundamental physiological properties (oscillatory dynamics, frequency, phase, state transitions) are lost, creating a blind spot that misranks models.
AI.method: TimeSynth introduces a controlled benchmarking framework with: 1) a physiologically grounded generator that produces signals with analytically known ground-truth dynamics from parametric models fitted to real EEG, ECG, and PPG signals, and 2) diagnostics quantifying amplitude, frequency, phase, and state-transition fidelity.
AI.result: Across 11 architectures, models with comparable pointwise error diverged by up to 53° in phase accuracy (equivalent to ~123 ms for 1.2 Hz cardiac rhythm). Linear and full-sequence attention models systematically lose frequency/phase information despite acceptable amplitude error, while architectures with localized temporal structure better preserve dynamical fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Architectural choice is the dominant determinant of fidelity, making model selection a principled, use-case-driven decision rather than a search for a single winner. TimeSynth provides the controlled preclinical stress test needed before coupling models to patient data.

title: Theoria: Rewrite-Acceptability Verification over Informal Reasoning States
summary: When should an AI system's answer be trusted? Formal proof assistants offer certainty but cannot reach most of the problem distribution; scalar LLM judges offer coverage but produce opaque scores that cannot be audited after the fact and are subject to the same coherence issues as any LLM. We present Theoria, a verification architecture that closes this gap. A candidate solution is rewritten into a sequence of typed state transitions, each licensed by an explicit justification, whether that be a citation, computation, or problem-given fact, and every transition is independently auditable. The foundational invariant is completeness of change: every difference between consecutive proof states must be accounted for, so hidden premises surface as unlicensed mutations rather than passing silently. On HLE-Verified Gold (185 text-only expert problems), Theoria certifies 105 at 91.4% strict precision (Wilson 95% CI [84.5%, 95.4%]). Every certification produces a human readable proof trace in which each step can be independently challenged. Holistic LLM judges achieve comparable precision at matched coverage but fail on different problems (Jaccard 0.14-0.36), making the approaches complementary. On 95 adversarial poisoned proofs across 15 domains, structured judges catch 94.7% versus 83.2% for holistic judging (p= 0.0017). The overall 11.5 pp gap concentrates in hidden premises (90.6% vs. 62.5%, a 28 pp difference) and fabricated citations (100% vs. 90%), the error classes where the formal analysis predicts an advantage; performance is identical on arithmetic and theorem-misapplication errors, where no advantage is predicted. On GPQA Diamond (n= 65), certified precision is 97.1% (Wilson CI [85.1%, 99.5%]).

AI.tldr: Theoria is a verification architecture that provides auditable, formal proofs for AI system outputs by breaking them into explicit, justified state transitions, offering both high coverage and verifiable certainty unlike traditional proof assistants or opaque LLM judges.
AI.motivation: Current approaches have trade-offs: formal proof assistants offer certainty but limited coverage, while scalar LLM judges offer coverage but produce opaque, unauditable scores that share LLM coherence issues. There's a gap between verifiable certainty and broad coverage.
AI.method: Theoria rewrites candidate solutions into sequences of typed state transitions, each requiring explicit justifications (citations, computations, or given facts). Every transition is independently auditable, with completeness of change as the foundational invariant - every difference between consecutive states must be accounted for, exposing hidden premises as unlicensed mutations.
AI.result: On HLE-Verified Gold (185 problems), Theoria certified 105 at 91.4% precision. Every certification produces human-readable proof traces. Outperforms holistic LLM judges on adversarial poisoned proofs (94.7% vs 83.2% detection), especially on hidden premises (90.6% vs 62.5%) and fabricated citations (100% vs 90%). On GPQA Diamond, certified precision is 97.1%.
AI.conclusion: Theoria bridges the gap between formal verification and broad coverage by providing auditable, structured proof verification that complements holistic LLM approaches. The method demonstrates strong performance on error detection, particularly for hidden premises and fabricated citations where its formal analysis framework provides clear advantages.

title: Gauging, Measuring, and Controlling Critic Complexity in Actor-Critic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Actor-critic methods depend on learned critics, but critic quality is often evaluated only indirectly through return, temporal-difference error, or value loss. Critic complexity is introduced as an additional diagnostic and intervention dimension for actor-critic reinforcement learning. The analysis uses spectral effective-rank entropy, a rank-like summary of the singular-value distributions of critic weight matrices, to assess critic model complexity. Across TD3 and PPO experiments, critic complexity is tracked together with return and Monte Carlo value-estimation bias. The results show that critic complexity is measurable throughout training and is systematically associated with training behavior, while also making clear that the relationship is heterogeneous across algorithms, tasks, and hyperparameters. A direct complexity-control intervention is then evaluated by adding a spectral-entropy penalty to the critic loss. This intervention reliably changes the targeted spectral quantity, demonstrating that critic complexity can be controlled rather than only observed. Return effects are treated as task-dependent evidence rather than as a general performance claim, because overall complexity-control results vary.

AI.tldr: Introduces critic complexity as a diagnostic dimension for actor-critic RL, shows it can be measured via spectral entropy, and demonstrates it can be controlled via penalties with variable performance effects.
AI.motivation: Current actor-critic methods lack direct measures of critic quality beyond indirect metrics like return or TD error. There's a need for better diagnostics and intervention dimensions specifically focused on critic complexity.
AI.method: Uses spectral effective-rank entropy to measure critic complexity from singular-value distributions of critic weight matrices. Tracks complexity alongside return and value-estimation bias in TD3 and PPO experiments. Evaluates a direct complexity-control intervention by adding a spectral-entropy penalty to critic loss.
AI.result: Critic complexity is measurable throughout training and systematically associated with training behavior, though relationships vary across algorithms, tasks, and hyperparameters. The complexity-control intervention reliably changes the targeted spectral quantity, showing complexity can be controlled, not just observed.
AI.conclusion: Critic complexity provides a valuable diagnostic dimension for actor-critic RL that can be both measured and controlled, though performance effects of complexity control are task-dependent rather than universally beneficial.

title: MolSafeEval: A Benchmark for Uncovering Safety Risks in AI-Generated Molecules
summary: Current molecular generation benchmarks emphasize task complexity, molecule novelty, and property alignment; they largely overlook a critical concern: the potential safety risks of AI-generated molecules. In practice, many generative models may produce molecules with toxic, reactive, or otherwise hazardous characteristics - posing hidden dangers that remain insufficiently addressed. To address this gap, we introduce MolSafeEval, a benchmark dedicated to evaluating and analyzing the safety risks of molecular generation. Unlike prior approaches that rely on narrow toxicity predictors, MolSafeEval integrates heterogeneous safety knowledge - ranging from toxicological databases to hazard rules - into a structured molecular safety knowledge graph. This graph serves as a foundation for large language model-based reasoning, enabling systematic detection and explanation of unsafe features in generated compounds. We further categorize molecular generative models into four representative task types - unconditional generation, property optimization, target protein-based design, and text-based generation - and provide standardized datasets and safety evaluation protocols for each. By systematically revealing the safety vulnerabilities of current generative approaches, MolSafeEval offers a new lens for benchmarking molecular models and provides essential guidance toward safer, more trustworthy molecular design.

AI.tldr: MolSafeEval is a benchmark for evaluating safety risks in AI-generated molecules, addressing overlooked hazardous characteristics by integrating heterogeneous safety knowledge into a structured knowledge graph and providing systematic safety evaluation protocols for different molecular generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Current molecular generation benchmarks largely overlook safety risks of AI-generated molecules. Many generative models may produce molecules with toxic, reactive, or hazardous characteristics, posing hidden dangers that remain insufficiently addressed in existing evaluation frameworks.
AI.method: MolSafeEval integrates heterogeneous safety knowledge from toxicological databases and hazard rules into a structured molecular safety knowledge graph. This graph enables systematic detection and explanation of unsafe features using large language model-based reasoning. The benchmark covers four molecular generative model types and provides standardized datasets and safety evaluation protocols for each.
AI.result: The paper introduces MolSafeEval as a comprehensive benchmark that systematically reveals safety vulnerabilities of current generative approaches, offering standardized evaluation protocols for unconditional generation, property optimization, target protein-based design, and text-based generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: MolSafeEval provides a new lens for benchmarking molecular models by addressing critical safety concerns, offering essential guidance toward safer and more trustworthy molecular design that has been overlooked in existing molecular generation benchmarks.

title: How Early Is Early Enough? Design-Dependent Observation-Window Sufficiency in Subscription Churn Prediction
summary: How many days of early behavior suffice for subscription churn prediction? In the public KKBox dataset, the early indicator of churn is typically an indicator of someone's contract status; however, when looking in the heavily churned manual-renewal segment, having access to early behavior creates a substantial increase in prediction for that specific segment (PR +0.10 at 120 days). A nine-window sufficiency curve shows a diminishing-returns knee in a 45-90 day band. However, stress-testing over three cohort/task designs shows that this curve is singular to the design being tested; for example, in our test with a moving target, the curve inverts and can shift depending on the feature set used. Therefore, any window-sufficiency claim should state its cohort construction, target definition, and feature families. All evidence is from one music-streaming dataset; the mechanism should generalize but the magnitudes may not.

AI.tldr: Early behavior (45-90 days) significantly improves churn prediction in manual-renewal segments, but optimal window size depends heavily on cohort construction, target definition, and feature choices.
AI.motivation: To determine how much early user behavior data is sufficient for effective subscription churn prediction, particularly examining the trade-off between prediction accuracy and data collection time windows.
AI.method: Analyzed the KKBox music streaming dataset using a nine-window sufficiency curve to examine diminishing returns. Conducted stress-testing across three different cohort/task designs (including moving target tests) with varying feature sets to evaluate robustness of findings.
AI.result: In manual-renewal segments (high churn), early behavior provides substantial prediction improvement (PR +0.10 at 120 days). A diminishing-returns knee occurs at 45-90 days, but optimal window size is highly dependent on design choices - the curve can invert or shift based on cohort construction and features used.
AI.conclusion: Window-sufficiency claims must specify cohort construction, target definition, and feature families. Early behavior (45-90 days) is valuable but optimal window size is context-dependent. Findings are based on music-streaming data but mechanisms likely generalize while magnitudes may not.

title: Interpretable vs Learned Encoders for High-Cardinality Fraud Detection
summary: A total of seven categorical encoding methods were tested on the IEEE-CIS fraud benchmark dataset (590,540 records, 3.5% positives, 8 high-cardinality columns). The encoders were evaluated using a stratified 5-fold cross-validation (CV) with three repetitions. Five of the encoders had identical frozen LightGBM learners in the downstream phase, allowing for controlled comparisons of their performance to each other. CatBoost and TabNet were included as comparisons across paradigms using different learners. The entity embeddings produced the highest AUC-ROC (0.9612), with a statistically significant tie with that of CatBoost (0.9602) and statistically superior to tier group encoding (0.9548), whereas target encoding was only 0.0023 worse than tier group encoding and the auditor-friendly tier boundaries were maintained. Off-the-shelf TabNet did not outperform tree-based pipelines and collapsed under data scarcity. On AUC-PR, CatBoost leads (0.822 vs. 0.793); no encoder dominated both metrics. Per-column analysis confirmed the embedding advantage arises from joint multi-column representation.

AI.tldr: Entity embeddings outperformed other categorical encoding methods for fraud detection on high-cardinality features, achieving best AUC-ROC (0.9612) with CatBoost performing similarly well on AUC-PR.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to systematically compare different categorical encoding methods for fraud detection, particularly focusing on high-cardinality categorical features where traditional encoding approaches may not be optimal.
AI.method: Tested seven categorical encoding methods on IEEE-CIS fraud dataset (590,540 records, 3.5% positives, 8 high-cardinality columns). Used stratified 5-fold cross-validation with three repetitions. Five encoders used identical frozen LightGBM learners, while CatBoost and TabNet were included as paradigm comparisons.
AI.result: Entity embeddings produced highest AUC-ROC (0.9612), statistically tied with CatBoost (0.9602) and superior to tier group encoding (0.9548). Target encoding performed close to tier group encoding. CatBoost led on AUC-PR (0.822 vs. 0.793). TabNet underperformed tree-based methods. Entity embedding advantage comes from joint multi-column representation.
AI.conclusion: Entity embeddings are highly effective for categorical encoding in fraud detection with high-cardinality features, while CatBoost performs well across different metrics. No single encoder dominates both AUC-ROC and AUC-PR metrics, suggesting method selection should consider the specific performance metric of interest.

title: Ghost in the Kernel: In-Context Learning with Efficient Transformers via Domain Generalization
summary: Transformer-based large models have demonstrated remarkable generalization abilities across different tasks by leveraging a context-aware attention module for in-context learning. With richer context, transformers adapt more effectively to the current use case without any parameter updates. However, the quadratic computational and memory complexity with respect to context length significantly slows data processing in softmax transformers. Linear transformers were proposed to address this issue by reducing the complexity to linear dependence on context length, but the design and understanding of the feature mapping in linear attention, from a theoretical viewpoint, remain unclear. In this paper, we investigate the approximation and generalization abilities of linear transformers under a two-staged sampling process from domain generalization. We show that linear transformers perform in-context learning as learning a mapping from context distributions to response functions. A dimension-independent convergence rate is obtained for our generalization analysis, which also exhibits the tradeoff between the regularities of data distributions and latent features. Guided by our theoretical framework, we propose a new perspective on activation and loss design for linearizing pretrained softmax large language models.

AI.tldr: Linear transformers address quadratic complexity issues of softmax transformers by reducing computational cost to linear dependence on context length, enabling more efficient in-context learning with theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: Transformers have remarkable generalization through attention mechanisms, but the quadratic computational and memory complexity with context length slows down processing. Linear transformers were proposed to address this, but theoretical understanding of their feature mapping and design principles remains unclear.
AI.method: The paper investigates linear transformers under a two-staged sampling process from domain generalization. It analyzes them as learning a mapping from context distributions to response functions, obtaining dimension-independent convergence rates and revealing tradeoffs between data distribution regularities and latent features.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows linear transformers perform in-context learning as distribution-to-function mapping, with dimension-independent convergence rates that reveal tradeoffs. This framework guides new perspectives on activation and loss design for linearizing pretrained softmax large language models.
AI.conclusion: Linear transformers provide theoretically-grounded alternatives to softmax transformers with linear computational complexity, enabling more efficient in-context learning while maintaining strong generalization capabilities, with implications for improving existing large language models.

title: PAPA: Online Personalized Active Preference Alignment
summary: Diffusion models are highly effective at modeling complex data distributions, including images and text. However, in applications like personalized recommender systems, the objective often shifts to modeling specific regions of the distribution that maximize user preferences-initially unknown but gradually uncovered through interactive feedback. This can naturally be framed as a reinforcement learning problem, where the goal is to fine-tune a diffusion model to maximize a reward function based on preferences. However, the main challenge lies in learning a parameterized reward model, which typically requires large-scale preference data-something that is often not feasible in practice. In this work, we introduce Personalized Active Preference Alignment PAPA, a novel method that bypasses the requirement for a parametrized reward model by directly optimizing the diffusion model using real-time user feedback. PAPA enables feedback-efficient preference alignment, drawing inspiration from the variational inference framework. We demonstrate PAPA's effectiveness through extensive experiments and ablation studies across diverse class-conditioned and fine-grained alignment tasks. Additionally, based on theoretical insights, we propose an enhanced fine-tuning strategy, referred to as EPAPA, that requires less computational budget and accelerates the fine-tuning process, further boosting PAPA's suitability for real-world deployment. Our code is made publicly available at https://github.com/NasikNafi/papa.

AI.tldr: PAPA (Personalized Active Preference Alignment) enables efficient preference alignment for diffusion models using direct user feedback without needing a parameterized reward model.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models excel at modeling complex data distributions, but applications like personalized recommender systems require modeling specific distribution regions that maximize user preferences. Existing reward-based RL approaches need large-scale preference data for reward models, which is often impractical.
AI.method: PAPA bypasses parameterized reward models by directly optimizing diffusion models using real-time user feedback. Inspired by variational inference, it enables feedback-efficient preference alignment. EPAPA is an enhanced version with improved computational efficiency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse class-conditioned and fine-grained alignment tasks demonstrate PAPA's effectiveness. EPAPA further reduces computational requirements and accelerates fine-tuning, enhancing real-world deployment suitability.
AI.conclusion: PAPA provides an effective framework for personalized preference alignment in diffusion models without needing large preference datasets. The method enables practical deployment in real-world applications through computational efficiency improvements in EPAPA.

title: Prototype Language Models
summary: Knowing which training examples drive outputs is fundamental to auditing, correcting, and understanding language models, yet for modern LLMs this remains expensive, approximate, and largely post-hoc. Standard language models generate tokens through a dense network pathway, causing training data's influence to be distributed across parameters rather than organized along explicit, traceable components. We introduce a prototype language model architecture, Prototypes for Interpretable Sequence Modeling (PRISM), that forms each prediction via a sparse, non-negative mixture of learned prototypes, trained with clustering objectives that anchor each prototype to coherent neighborhoods of training examples. Across architectures from 130M to 1.6B parameters trained on up to 50B tokens, prototype language models either surpass or remain within 2.5 percentage points on average downstream accuracy of matched dense baselines. We show that sparse prototype structure localizes curvature in the loss landscape, yielding a more tractable Hessian and enabling training data attribution that is ~500x faster than post hoc baselines when consuming equivalent memory. Calibrating linear prototype controllers can improve downstream accuracy by roughly 3 points while tracing those corrections back to training neighborhoods, and targeted prototype suppression can remove model behaviors without finetuning or measurable loss in generation quality.

AI.tldr: PRISM introduces a prototype-based language model architecture that uses sparse, interpretable prototypes instead of dense parameter mixing, achieving similar performance to standard LLMs while enabling fast training data attribution and interpretable control.
AI.motivation: Current language models make it difficult to trace which training examples influence specific outputs - attribution is expensive, approximate, and post-hoc. Dense parameter networks distribute training data influence broadly rather than organizing it along traceable components.
AI.method: PRISM uses a prototype-based architecture where predictions are formed via sparse, non-negative mixtures of learned prototypes. Prototypes are trained with clustering objectives that anchor each prototype to coherent neighborhoods of training examples, creating interpretable components.
AI.result: PRISM models from 130M to 1.6B parameters match or slightly trail (within 2.5%) dense baselines on downstream accuracy. The sparse structure enables ~500x faster training data attribution, improves interpretability, and allows targeted behavior modification without finetuning or quality loss.
AI.conclusion: Prototype-based architectures offer a viable path toward more interpretable language models that maintain performance while enabling practical training data attribution, interpretable control mechanisms, and targeted behavior modification without compromising generation quality.

title: From Structural Equation Modelling to Double Machine Learning: Robustness Analysis for Survey-Based Research
summary: Structural equation modelling (SEM) is widely used in survey-based business and information systems research to assess latent constructs and theory-driven structural relationships. However, SEM path significance is obtained within a particular model specification and may not show whether findings remain stable under alternative estimation frameworks. This study develops and demonstrates a staged robustness analysis framework that connects SEM, ordinary least squares (OLS) regression, and Double Machine Learning (DML). SEM is first used to refine the measurement structure and estimate the robustness-baseline SEM model, in which the full theory-specified structural path system is retained for downstream robustness analysis before final structural path evaluation. OLS regression is then applied to SEM-derived construct scores as a transparent regression benchmark. Finally, DML-style residualisation is used to examine whether each tested focal relationship remains stable after flexible machine-learning-based adjustment for observed controls. Learner-sensitivity checks compare Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Support Vector Machine learners, and selected reverse-direction diagnostics are used to examine directional sensitivity. The framework is demonstrated using a FinTech Digital Customer Intimacy survey model. The findings identify which relationships are stable across SEM, OLS, and DML-style checks, and which require more cautious interpretation. A reproducible Google Colab workbook and generated result files are publicly available, providing a reusable template that researchers and students can adapt to other survey-based latent-construct studies. The paper contributes a practical robustness workflow and interpretation guide for survey-based researchers seeking to complement SEM with conventional and machine-learning-based robustness checks.

AI.tldr: Develops a staged robustness analysis framework combining SEM, OLS regression, and Double Machine Learning for testing stability of survey-based research findings across different estimation methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional SEM provides path significance within a specific model specification but doesn't show whether findings remain stable under alternative estimation frameworks. Researchers need ways to verify that their results are robust to different analytical approaches.
AI.method: Three-stage framework: 1) SEM for measurement refinement and baseline estimation; 2) OLS regression on SEM-derived construct scores as transparent benchmark; 3) DML-style residualisation with flexible ML-based adjustment for controls, comparing Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Support Vector Machine learners with directional sensitivity diagnostics.
AI.result: Framework successfully identifies which relationships are stable across SEM, OLS, and DML-style checks, and which require more cautious interpretation. Demonstrated using a FinTech Digital Customer Intimacy survey model, with publicly available reproducible workbook and result files.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical robustness workflow and interpretation guide for survey researchers to complement SEM with conventional and machine-learning-based robustness checks, enhancing confidence in findings by testing stability across multiple analytical frameworks.

title: Active-GRPO: Adaptive Imitation and Self-Improving Reasoning for Molecular Optimization
summary: Scientific reasoning is an increasingly important capability of large language models, yet improving the robustness and efficiency of training such reasoning remains a key open challenge. We study this problem in instruction-based molecular optimization, where answer-only supervised fine-tuning (SFT) collapses multi-step reasoning and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) suffers from sparse feedback. Reference-guided Policy Optimization mitigates both by anchoring policy updates to dataset-provided references, but its effectiveness is tightly coupled to reference quality: weak or misaligned references impose a performance ceiling. To overcome this ceiling, we propose active reasoning, a paradigm in which the policy actively decides, on a per-instance basis, when to imitate a reference and when to reinforce its own discoveries, while continuously upgrading what it imitates. We instantiate this paradigm as Active Group Relative Policy Optimization (Active-GRPO), realized through two coupled mechanisms: active imitate-reinforce and active referencing. The former performs imitation learning when the reference still outperforms the policy's own candidates, and shifts to self-improvement via reinforcement learning once the policy has generated molecules that surpass the reference. The latter continuously upgrades the reference itself by replacing it with the best policy-generated candidate discovered so far, progressively raising the imitation target and ensuring that reference guidance remains informative-rather than restrictive-throughout training. Across TOMG-Bench MOLOPT, Active-GRPO improves average SRxSim from 0.0959 for GRPO and 0.1665 for RePO to 0.1773 under matched three-seed evaluation, with statistically significant gains on LogP, MR, and QED.

AI.tldr: Active-GRPO improves molecular optimization by allowing the policy to actively decide when to imitate references vs. self-improve via reinforcement learning, while continuously upgrading references with the best discovered candidates.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to scientific reasoning in molecular optimization have limitations: answer-only supervised fine-tuning collapses multi-step reasoning, reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards suffers from sparse feedback, and reference-guided methods are constrained by reference quality.
AI.method: Proposes Active Group Relative Policy Optimization (Active-GRPO) with two coupled mechanisms: 1) active imitate-reinforce - policy decides per-instance whether to imitate references or self-improve via RL, 2) active referencing - continuously upgrades references with best policy-generated candidates to maintain informative guidance.
AI.result: On TOMG-Bench MOLOPT, Active-GRPO improves average SRxSim from 0.0959 (GRPO) and 0.1665 (RePO) to 0.1773, with statistically significant gains on LogP, MR, and QED metrics.
AI.conclusion: The active reasoning paradigm, implemented as Active-GRPO, effectively overcomes limitations of existing approaches by dynamically balancing imitation and self-improvement while progressively upgrading reference quality, leading to better molecular optimization performance.

title: Flow-Map GRPO: Reinforcement Learning for Few-Step Flow-Map Generators via Anchored Stochastic Composition
summary: Few-step flow-map generators, such as consistency models and MeanFlow, accelerate sampling by directly learning long-range transport maps between noise and data. However, these models are typically deterministic, which makes them difficult to optimize with reinforcement learning (RL) post-training methods that require stochastic trajectories and well-defined likelihood ratios. Existing SDE-based stochasticization techniques are designed for velocity-based samplers with infinitesimal or finely discretized transitions, and therefore do not directly apply to long-range flow maps. In this work, we propose Flow-Map GRPO, an online RL post-training framework for deterministic few-step flow-map generators. The key component is Anchored Stochastic Flow Map Composition (ASFMC), a path-preserving stochasticization mechanism that introduces randomness through anchor-based conditional resampling while preserving the original marginal probability path of the deterministic flow map. We derive GRPO objectives for both single-time and two-time flow-map parameterizations. Experiments on few-step FLUX-based text-to-image generators, including MeanFlow and sCM, show that Flow-Map GRPO improves pretrained deterministic flow-map models across reward-based, perceptual, and task-level evaluation metrics. Our results demonstrate that deterministic few-step flow-map generators can be effectively aligned with RL post-training without modifying their original model parameterization or retraining them as native stochastic models.

AI.tldr: Flow-Map GRPO enables reinforcement learning post-training for deterministic few-step flow-map generators (like consistency models) using a novel stochasticization method called ASFMC, allowing RL alignment without modifying original model architecture.
AI.motivation: Deterministic few-step flow-map generators (e.g., consistency models, MeanFlow) are efficient for fast sampling but cannot be optimized with RL post-training methods, which require stochastic trajectories and likelihood ratios for policy gradient updates.
AI.method: Proposes Flow-Map GRPO framework with Anchored Stochastic Flow Map Composition (ASFMC) - a path-preserving stochasticization mechanism that introduces randomness through anchor-based conditional resampling while maintaining original marginal probability paths. Derives GRPO objectives for single-time and two-time flow-map parameterizations.
AI.result: Experiments on few-step FLUX-based text-to-image generators (MeanFlow, sCM) show Flow-Map GRPO improves pretrained deterministic flow-map models across reward-based, perceptual, and task-level evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic few-step flow-map generators can be effectively aligned with RL post-training without modifying their original parameterization or retraining as native stochastic models, enabling RL benefits for efficient sampling models.

title: Group-Equivariant Poincaré Convolutional Networks
summary: While recent advancements like the Poincaré ResNet have demonstrated the potential of learning visual representations directly in hyperbolic space, their optimisation remains hampered by the computationally intensive nature of Riemannian gradients and the strict boundaries of the manifold. Furthermore, standard hyperbolic networks treat spatial transformations of the same object as distinct hierarchical concepts, leading to redundant parameter usage and vanishing signals. We propose Equivariant Poincaré ResNets, combining hyperbolic geometry with discrete symmetry groups ($C_4$ and $D_4$). We identify critical roadblocks in applying Euclidean equivariance to hyperbolic space and propose geometrically safe tensor reshaping, left-regular permutations for hyperbolic group convolutions, and joint-orientation Poincaré Midpoint Batch normalisation. Empirically, embedding equivariance drastically reduces the optimisation space, accelerating convergence while accelerating convergence while respecting the boundary constraints of the Poincaré ball and preserving spatial-group equivariance.

AI.tldr: Equivariant Poincaré ResNets combine hyperbolic geometry with symmetry groups to address optimization challenges and redundant parameter usage in hyperbolic networks.
AI.motivation: Recent hyperbolic networks like Poincaré ResNet face optimization difficulties due to computationally intensive Riemannian gradients and manifold boundary constraints. They also inefficiently treat spatial transformations of the same object as distinct hierarchical concepts, leading to parameter redundancy and vanishing signals.
AI.method: Propose Equivariant Poincaré ResNets that combine hyperbolic geometry with discrete symmetry groups (C₄ and D₄). Introduce geometrically safe tensor reshaping, left-regular permutations for hyperbolic group convolutions, and joint-orientation Poincaré Midpoint Batch normalization to address challenges in applying Euclidean equivariance to hyperbolic space.
AI.result: Embedding equivariance drastically reduces the optimization space and accelerates convergence while respecting the Poincaré ball boundary constraints and preserving spatial-group equivariance.
AI.conclusion: The combination of hyperbolic geometry with symmetry groups through Equivariant Poincaré ResNets successfully addresses the key limitations of existing hyperbolic networks, improving optimization efficiency and reducing parameter redundancy while maintaining geometric constraints.

title: Decision-focused Sparse Tangent Portfolio Optimization
summary: Sparse tangent portfolio optimization aims to learn an interpretable, low-cardinality portfolio in the tangency direction of the mean-variance frontier. However, the associated cardinality-constrained formulation is NP-hard, and standard predict-then-optimize pipelines often misalign forecasting accuracy with downstream portfolio quality. We propose an end-to-end decision-focused learning framework that reformulates Sharpe ratio maximization as a Disciplined Parametrized Programming (DPP)-compliant convex programming layer and replaces discrete selection with a smooth top-$k$ operator enforcing an exact cardinality $k$. This enables gradient flow through prediction, asset selection, and re-optimization, allowing the predictive model to directly optimize portfolio performance. Across four major equity markets, our method achieves competitive and often superior out-of-sample Sharpe ratios compared with historical and prediction-focused baselines, with particularly strong gains in larger asset universes. Our \href{https://github.com/feuerwerksh/Diffble-card-SR}{code} is publicly available.

AI.tldr: Sparse tangent portfolio optimization framework that uses end-to-end decision-focused learning with differentiable convex programming and smooth top-k selection to directly optimize portfolio performance through gradient flow.
AI.motivation: Sparse tangent portfolio optimization is NP-hard due to cardinality constraints, and standard predict-then-optimize approaches suffer from misalignment between forecasting accuracy and actual portfolio performance.
AI.method: Proposes an end-to-end decision-focused learning framework that reformulates Sharpe ratio maximization as a DPP-compliant convex programming layer, replaces discrete selection with smooth top-k operator for exact cardinality enforcement, and enables gradient flow through prediction, selection, and re-optimization.
AI.result: Achieves competitive/superior out-of-sample Sharpe ratios across four major equity markets compared to historical and prediction-focused baselines, with particularly strong gains in larger asset universes.
AI.conclusion: The end-to-end approach successfully aligns prediction and optimization objectives, enabling direct optimization of portfolio performance through differentiable programming, with code publicly available for reproducibility.

title: Measuring Dead Directions: Decomposing and Classifying Singular Structure off Canonical Alignment
comment: 45 pages, 14 figures, 19 tables. Methods and empirical companion to arXiv:2606.05957 (Dead Directions: Geometric Singular Learning)
summary: We give a descent-free, alignment-free measurement of singular structure on trained networks. At a single frozen checkpoint the read recovers the order $k$ of each dead direction from the directional-Fisher rate, the master invariant from which the per-direction learning coefficient $1/(2k)$ follows exactly, in whatever basis the optimizer left. The same read classifies each direction, separating a genuine singularity, whose order the architecture fixes, from a flat gauge symmetry; the directional-Fisher magnitude settles the cases the order cannot. A pluggable detector supplies the directions for transformer, convolutional, and normalisation layers. The read recovers the architecture-predicted order across constructed cells and trained networks, including a fine-tuned vision transformer whose dead structure is the LayerNorm-kernel gauge and a from-scratch one whose compressed MLP forms a node-death at its activation order. Where the singular structure enumerates, the per-direction orders assemble, through the typed intersection of the loci, into the global coefficient $(λ, m)$ matching the closed form. The method removes the canonical-alignment and descent preconditions of the underlying rate result, turning order-recovery into a deterministic, architecture-general reading. We then map its reach into the Watanabe triple: the order determines the universal singular fluctuation $ν(k)$, though a trained network's realized $ν$ falls below it as the live structure absorbs the dead direction's data fluctuation, and the multiplicity recovers from the dominant structure under a single-locus assumption.

AI.tldr: A method to measure singular structure in trained neural networks without descent or alignment, recovering learning coefficients and classifying dead directions from frozen network checkpoints.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for analyzing singular structure in neural networks require optimization descent or canonical alignment, limiting their applicability. The paper aims to develop a more general, architecture-agnostic approach that works on frozen trained networks.
AI.method: Uses directional-Fisher rate analysis at a single frozen checkpoint to recover order k of dead directions and learning coefficients. Includes a pluggable direction detector for transformer, convolutional, and normalization layers, separating genuine singularities from gauge symmetries.
AI.result: The method successfully recovers architecture-predicted orders across constructed cells and trained networks, including vision transformers and MLPs. It determines universal singular fluctuations and recovers multiplicities under single-locus assumptions.
AI.conclusion: The approach removes canonical-alignment and descent requirements, turning order recovery into a deterministic, architecture-general reading method that extends the reach into Watanabe's triple of singular learning theory.

title: Loss Smoothing for Stable Adaptation Under Distribution Shift
summary: In settings such as fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, neural networks are often adapted under distribution shift. Standard adaptation methods typically optimize the target objective directly, inducing an abrupt change from the source training objective. This abrupt transition can distort learned representations, including features that may still be useful for the new task. We investigate whether a more gradual transition can improve adaptation. We propose loss smoothing, a simple approach that interpolates between the source and target training objectives at the start of adaptation. This smooth transition helps to preserve useful features from the source distribution while still enabling the model to specialize to the target distribution. Across controlled supervised shifts, pretrained vision adaptation, offline-to-online and online reinforcement learning, and language model fine-tuning, we find that loss smoothing consistently improves performance, suggesting that smoother objective transitions are a broadly useful tool for model adaptation.

AI.tldr: Loss smoothing - gradually interpolating between source and target objectives during adaptation - improves performance across multiple domains by preserving useful features while enabling specialization.
AI.motivation: Standard adaptation methods abruptly switch from source to target objectives, which can distort learned representations and potentially lose useful features that could still benefit the new task.
AI.method: Proposes "loss smoothing" - a simple approach that interpolates between the source and target training objectives at the start of adaptation, creating a gradual transition rather than an abrupt switch.
AI.result: Across multiple domains including controlled supervised shifts, pretrained vision adaptation, offline-to-online and online reinforcement learning, and language model fine-tuning, loss smoothing consistently improves adaptation performance.
AI.conclusion: Smoother objective transitions are a broadly useful tool for model adaptation, helping preserve valuable features from source distributions while enabling effective specialization to target distributions.

title: Multi-Label Node Classification with Label Influence Propagation
summary: Graphs are a complex and versatile data structure used across various domains, with possibly multi-label nodes playing a particularly crucial role. Examples include proteins in PPI networks with multiple functions and users in social or e-commerce networks exhibiting diverse interests. Tackling multi-label node classification (MLNC) on graphs has led to the development of various approaches. Some methods leverage graph neural networks (GNNs) to exploit label co-occurrence correlations, while others incorporate label embeddings to capture label proximity. However, these approaches fail to account for the intricate influences between labels in non-Euclidean graph data. To address this issue, we decompose the message passing process in GNNs into two operations: propagation and transformation. We then conduct a comprehensive analysis and quantification of the influence correlations between labels in each operation. Building on these insights, we propose a novel model, Label Influence Propagation (LIP). Specifically, we construct a label influence graph based on the integrated label correlations. Then, we propagate high-order influences through this graph, dynamically adjusting the learning process by amplifying labels with positive contributions and mitigating those with negative influence. Finally, our framework is evaluated on comprehensive benchmark datasets, consistently outperforming SOTA methods across various settings, demonstrating its effectiveness on MLNC tasks.

AI.tldr: Proposes Label Influence Propagation (LIP), a novel graph neural network approach for multi-label node classification that captures and propagates complex label correlations through a dedicated label influence graph.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-label node classification methods using GNNs fail to adequately capture the intricate influence relationships between labels in non-Euclidean graph data, particularly for nodes with multiple labels like proteins in PPI networks or users in social networks.
AI.method: The paper decomposes GNN message passing into propagation and transformation operations, analyzes label influence correlations, constructs a label influence graph based on integrated label correlations, propagates high-order influences through this graph, and dynamically adjusts learning by amplifying positively contributing labels and mitigating negative influences.
AI.result: The proposed Label Influence Propagation (LIP) model consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on comprehensive benchmark datasets across various settings.
AI.conclusion: The LIP framework effectively addresses the limitations of existing approaches by explicitly modeling and propagating label influence correlations, demonstrating superior performance for multi-label node classification tasks on graphs.

title: Distributed Online Bandit Submodular Maximization with Bounded Sampling Violations
summary: We study distributed online submodular maximization under partition matroid constraints, in which multiple agents select a limited number of actions from their own subsets sequentially to maximize the cumulative value of a sequence of objective functions. We develop a unified algorithmic framework that accommodates full-information and bandit feedback models. For both feedback models, we prove that the proposed algorithms achieve sublinear $(1-1/e)$-regret guarantees, which are comparable to those achieved by existing centralized counterparts. Furthermore, to tackle the sampling violation issue caused by continuous relaxation and rounding, we develop a bounded stochastic pipage rounding scheme and show that the probability of sampling violation vanishes asymptotically. As a result, the cumulative sampling violation remains sublinear in $T$, which is further shown to be not improvable under certain conditions. Numerical results validate the theoretical findings in this paper.

AI.tldr: Distributed online submodular maximization under partition matroid constraints with sublinear (1-1/e)-regret guarantees for both full-information and bandit feedback models.
AI.motivation: Address distributed online optimization where multiple agents sequentially select limited actions from their own subsets to maximize cumulative value of objective functions, with applications in resource allocation, sensor placement, and influence maximization.
AI.method: Develop unified algorithmic framework for full-information and bandit feedback models, incorporating bounded stochastic pipage rounding to address sampling violation issues from continuous relaxation and rounding.
AI.result: Achieve sublinear (1-1/e)-regret comparable to centralized counterparts for both feedback models, with sampling violation probability vanishing asymptotically and cumulative violation remaining sublinear in T, shown to be not improvable under certain conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed distributed algorithms effectively solve online submodular maximization under partition matroid constraints with strong theoretical guarantees, validated by numerical results.

title: AdaBoosting Text Prompts for Vision-Language Models
summary: The classification accuracy of pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) relies on the quality of the text prompts. Handcrafted templates and Large Language Model (LLM)-generated descriptions not only make predictions more interpretable, but also enable reuse of the same prompts across heterogeneous VLMs. Recent works construct task-adapted text prompts with a small number of labeled images. However, existing few-shot text prompting methods do not explicitly focus on misclassified examples during prompt construction, leading to only marginal improvements even as more shots become available. To fully exploit few-shot supervision, we propose Text Prompt Boosting (TPB), an AdaBoost-inspired framework that treats each text-prompt-based classifier as a weak learner and sequentially aggregates them into a strong ensemble by explicitly targeting hard, misclassified examples. Extensive experiments show that TPB preserves task-intrinsic, model-agnostic cues in text space, enabling robust cross-model transfer. Across eleven classification benchmarks, TPB improves accuracy on the source model and preserves shot-driven gains when transferred to larger, more capable VLMs, where existing methods struggle to sustain such improvements.

AI.tldr: TPB (Text Prompt Boosting) is an AdaBoost-inspired framework that treats text-prompt-based classifiers as weak learners and sequentially aggregates them by targeting misclassified examples, enabling robust cross-model transfer in vision-language models.
AI.motivation: Existing few-shot text prompting methods for Vision-Language Models don't explicitly focus on misclassified examples during prompt construction, leading to only marginal improvements even with more shots. There's a need to better exploit few-shot supervision by targeting hard examples to improve classification accuracy.
AI.method: Proposes Text Prompt Boosting (TPB), an AdaBoost-inspired framework that treats each text-prompt-based classifier as a weak learner and sequentially aggregates them into a strong ensemble. The method explicitly targets hard, misclassified examples during prompt construction to fully exploit few-shot supervision.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across eleven classification benchmarks show TPB improves accuracy on the source model and preserves shot-driven gains when transferred to larger, more capable VLMs. TPB preserves task-intrinsic, model-agnostic cues in text space, enabling robust cross-model transfer where existing methods struggle.
AI.conclusion: TPB effectively addresses the limitation of existing few-shot text prompting methods by focusing on misclassified examples, enabling better utilization of few-shot supervision and achieving robust performance improvements that transfer well across different VLMs.

title: Generative Refinement for Low-Budget Black-Box Optimization
summary: Black-box optimization is a fundamental science and engineering tool that makes it possible to optimize objectives without gradient information. Unfortunately, as it often requires many function evaluations, it can be challenging when each one is costly. This is especially true when the evaluation function is noisy or failure-prone, and when high-performing solutions are confined to thin, curved, or disconnected regions of the search space. Existing methods leveraging generative models to navigate these subspaces are built to sample from reward-aligned distributions. As a result, they require a large number of evaluations to align their sampler effectively, making them impractical in low-budget settings. We propose SPARROW, an algorithm that completely decouples the generative prior from the reward signal. SPARROW can use any sampler with a known corruption process and trained on unevaluated data, as a fixed, structured proposal operator. Optimization proceeds by rank-based guidance over an archive of evaluated candidates. SPARROW can navigate complex geometries, handle unreliable reward signals, and perform effective optimization under very low evaluation budgets. We provide asymptotic convergence guarantees over the sampler support and demonstrate strong empirical performance on problems with unreliable rewards and geometrically complex landscapes.

AI.tldr: SPARROW is a black-box optimization algorithm that decouples generative priors from reward signals, enabling effective optimization with very low evaluation budgets even in complex, noisy search spaces.
AI.motivation: Black-box optimization is challenging when function evaluations are costly, especially with noisy/failure-prone functions and complex search spaces where high-performing solutions are confined to thin, curved, or disconnected regions. Existing generative model methods require too many evaluations to align their samplers with rewards, making them impractical for low-budget settings.
AI.method: SPARROW completely decouples the generative prior from the reward signal. It uses any sampler with a known corruption process (trained on unevaluated data) as a fixed, structured proposal operator. Optimization proceeds through rank-based guidance over an archive of evaluated candidates.
AI.result: SPARROW demonstrates strong empirical performance on problems with unreliable rewards and geometrically complex landscapes. It can navigate complex geometries, handle unreliable reward signals, and perform effective optimization under very low evaluation budgets.
AI.conclusion: SPARROW provides an effective solution for black-box optimization in challenging low-budget settings by decoupling generative priors from reward signals, with asymptotic convergence guarantees over the sampler support and practical advantages over existing methods.

title: Detecting the Undetectable: Enhancing Unsupervised time series Anomaly Detection via Active Learning
summary: Despite the increasing sophistication of industrial AI systems, the ability to reliably detect subtle and noisy anomalies in complex time series data remains a critical yet unresolved challenge.
