AI.tldr: GSRQ is a new KV cache quantization method that improves directional fidelity in LLMs by replacing standard K-means with Gain-Shape K-means, achieving substantial accuracy improvements at low bit rates.
AI.motivation: LLM deployment with extended context windows is constrained by linear KV cache memory growth. While Vector Quantization (especially Residual Quantization) offers sub-1-bit compression, existing methods using standard K-means suffer from centroid shrinkage in high dimensions, weakening angular alignment and directional preservation.
AI.method: Proposes Gain-Shape K-means (GSKM) as a drop-in replacement for standard K-means, addressing centroid shrinkage by improving directional fidelity while maintaining good ℓ₂ distortion. Then builds Gain-Shape Residual Quantization (GSRQ) by incorporating weighted GSKM into an RQ pipeline for KV cache quantization.
AI.result: GSRQ substantially outperforms strong KV cache quantization baselines across bit rates on LLaMA-3-8B. At 1-bit, it improves average LongBench accuracy from 11.34 to 33.54 (22.20 percentage points gain over VQLLM).
AI.conclusion: The proposed GSKM addresses fundamental limitations of Euclidean centroid averaging in high dimensions, enabling more effective KV cache compression through GSRQ with significant accuracy improvements, particularly at extremely low bit rates like 1-bit.

title: Balancing Expressivity and Learnability in Quantum Kernel Bandit Optimization
summary: We investigate Gaussian process (GP) bandit optimization with quantum kernels, assuming the mean reward function lies in the reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) induced by the quantum kernel. This setting is motivated by NISQ-era tasks such as quantum control, state preparation and variational quantum algorithms. While quantum kernels can offer a `quantum advantage' via domain-specific inductive biases, naïvely using full, high-dimensional kernels increases model complexity and information gain, leading to higher cumulative regret and poor learnability. To address this, we propose projected quantum kernels and classical kernel approximation techniques that reduce feature dimensionality while preserving key quantum properties. Using these approximate kernels, we develop misspecified GP bandit algorithms and derive regret bounds that characterize the trade-off between approximation error and information gain. The regret bounds provide principled guidance for selecting the optimal model complexity. Empirically, our methods outperform full quantum kernels in sample efficiency, while substantially reducing computational overhead, enabling scalable GP optimization for quantum-native applications.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes projected quantum kernels and classical approximations to reduce dimensionality in Gaussian process bandit optimization with quantum kernels, achieving better sample efficiency and scalability than full quantum kernels.
AI.motivation: Quantum kernels offer potential 'quantum advantage' for NISQ-era tasks like quantum control and variational algorithms, but full high-dimensional quantum kernels increase model complexity and information gain, leading to poor learnability and high cumulative regret in GP bandit optimization.
AI.method: The authors propose two approaches: 1) projected quantum kernels that reduce feature dimensionality while preserving quantum properties, and 2) classical kernel approximation techniques. They develop misspecified GP bandit algorithms using these approximate kernels and derive regret bounds analyzing the trade-off between approximation error and information gain.
AI.result: Theoretical regret bounds characterize the trade-off between approximation error and information gain, providing principled guidance for optimal model complexity selection. Empirical results show their methods outperform full quantum kernels in sample efficiency while substantially reducing computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Projected quantum kernels and classical approximations enable scalable Gaussian process optimization for quantum-native applications by reducing dimensionality while maintaining key quantum properties, offering a practical solution to the scalability issues of full quantum kernels in GP bandit optimization.

title: When Context Compensates for Sparse Event History: AlphaEarth for Spatio-Temporal Point-Process Forecasting
summary: Spatio-temporal point-process models must often generalise across space when local event histories are sparse. We study whether exogenous spatial context can compensate in such regimes. Using a fixed log-Gaussian Cox process backbone, we compare an event-only model with the same model augmented by AlphaEarth embeddings as linear spatial context. We evaluate spatial transfer on emergency medical services (EMS) forecasting across eight held-out regions, fixed forecast anchors, and a sweep over history length $w$, using only AlphaEarth (AE) embeddings available strictly before each anchor. AE improves out-of-region predictive performance across all history regimes, with the largest gains under scarce histories: approximately $2$--$6\times$ multiplicative improvements at $1-2$ weeks, tapering to roughly $10$--$20\%$ at $w=20$--$104$ weeks. These results show that contextual information can substantially stabilise spatially transferred point-process forecasts when event history is limited.

AI.tldr: AlphaEarth spatial embeddings improve spatio-temporal point process forecasting for EMS data, especially when local event history is sparse, with up to 2-6× improvement for short histories and 10-20% for longer histories.
AI.motivation: Spatio-temporal point-process models often need to generalize across space when local event histories are sparse, requiring compensation for limited historical data to improve forecasting accuracy.
AI.method: Used a fixed log-Gaussian Cox process backbone and compared an event-only model with the same model augmented by AlphaEarth embeddings as linear spatial context. Evaluated spatial transfer across eight held-out regions with fixed forecast anchors, sweeping over history length w, using only AlphaEarth embeddings available before each anchor.
AI.result: AlphaEarth embeddings improve out-of-region predictive performance across all history regimes, with largest gains under scarce histories: approximately 2-6× multiplicative improvements at 1-2 weeks, tapering to roughly 10-20% at w=20-104 weeks.
AI.conclusion: Contextual information from spatial embeddings can substantially stabilize spatially transferred point-process forecasts when event history is limited, demonstrating the value of spatial context compensation in sparse history regimes.

title: SynLaD: Latent Diffusion for Generating Synthesizable Molecules Conditioned on 3D Pharmacophore Profiles
summary: We present SynLaD, a latent diffusion framework for small-molecule generation that unifies ligand-based drug design objectives (what to make) with synthetic accessibility (how to make it). Current models typically optimize one objective at the expense of the other, creating a bottleneck for discovering high-scoring and synthesizable molecules. SynLaD combines reaction-constrained generation with pharmacophore-conditioned 3D design by learning a latent space that decodes to both 3D structures and synthesis pathways. An encoder maps molecules to a latent representation used by two decoder heads: (i) a geometric head that reconstructs atom types and coordinates and (ii) an autoregressive synthesis head that outputs synthetic routes in a serialized, reaction-based notation. A diffusion transformer generates novel latents in the learned space, conditioned on pharmacophore profiles. Across analogue generation tasks for bioactive ligands, SynLaD outperforms existing baselines in synthesizable and diverse hit generation, demonstrating that a single model can produce shape-aligned molecules with feasible synthesis plans.

AI.tldr: SynLaD is a unified latent diffusion framework that generates synthesizable 3D small molecules by combining ligand-based drug design objectives with synthetic accessibility constraints through a dual-decoder architecture.
AI.motivation: Current molecular generation models typically optimize either pharmacophore objectives (what to make) or synthetic accessibility (how to make it), but not both simultaneously. This creates a bottleneck for discovering molecules that are both high-scoring (biologically active) and synthesizable in practice.
AI.method: SynLaD learns a latent space that decodes to both 3D structures and synthesis pathways. It uses an encoder to map molecules to latent representations, with two decoder heads: (1) a geometric head that reconstructs atom types and coordinates for 3D structure, and (2) an autoregressive synthesis head that outputs synthetic routes in reaction-based notation. A diffusion transformer generates novel latents conditioned on pharmacophore profiles.
AI.result: Across analogue generation tasks for bioactive ligands, SynLaD outperforms existing baselines in synthesizable and diverse hit generation. The model successfully produces shape-aligned molecules with feasible synthesis plans, demonstrating that a single model can effectively address both design objectives simultaneously.
AI.conclusion: SynLaD provides a unified framework that bridges the gap between ligand-based drug design and synthetic chemistry by combining reaction-constrained generation with pharmacophore-conditioned 3D design, enabling the generation of both biologically relevant and practically synthesizable molecules.

title: ZO-Act: Efficient Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning via One-Shot Activation-Informed Low-Rank Subspaces
summary: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization enables fine-tuning large language models when backpropagation is unavailable or memory-prohibitive, but existing methods often perturb full model weights or randomly constructed low-dimensional subspaces, yielding high-variance estimates and limited performance. We propose ZO-Act, an activation-informed ZO fine-tuning method that restricts perturbations to a fixed low-rank subspace derived from input activations. For each linear layer, ZO-Act computes a small activation basis once at initialization and optimizes only lightweight coefficient matrices using forward-only loss evaluations. This reduces the effective perturbation dimension, exposes explicit trainable variables compatible with momentum-based optimizers such as Adam, and naturally supports quantized LLM fine-tuning by keeping low-bit weights frozen. We analyze ZO-Act as zeroth-order optimization over a restricted coefficient space and show that perturbing the low-dimensional coefficients reduces both the variance-dependent convergence term and the finite-difference error of the ZO estimator, at the cost of a controlled subspace approximation bias that is mitigated by the low-rank structure of LLM activations and gradients. Experiments on Llama-3-8B, OPT-13B, and INT4 Llama-3-8B show consistent gains over strong ZO fine-tuning baselines across language understanding, question answering, and commonsense reasoning.

AI.tldr: ZO-Act enables efficient zeroth-order LLM fine-tuning by restricting perturbations to low-rank activation subspaces, reducing variance and supporting quantization while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Current ZO optimization methods for LLM fine-tuning suffer from high variance due to full model weight perturbations or random low-dimensional subspaces, leading to limited performance and memory inefficiency.
AI.method: ZO-Act restricts perturbations to a fixed low-rank subspace derived from input activations, computing small activation bases once at initialization and optimizing only lightweight coefficient matrices via forward-only loss evaluations.
AI.result: ZO-Act achieves consistent performance gains over strong ZO baselines on Llama-3-8B, OPT-13B, and INT4 Llama-3-8B across language understanding, QA, and commonsense reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: The method reduces perturbation dimension variance while controlling subspace bias, enables momentum-based optimization and quantized LLM fine-tuning, and demonstrates superior practical performance.

title: GAIA: Geometry-Adaptive Operator Learning for Forward and Inverse Problems
summary: Operator learning for partial differential equations (PDEs) on arbitrary geometries builds fast neural surrogates for large-scale simulation. Although recent geometry-adaptive neural operators have made substantial progress, they are mainly designed for forward problems in which inputs and outputs share the same spatial domain. This limits their applicability for boundary value problems (BVPs) and inverse problems, where inputs and outputs may live on different domains. We introduce the Geometry-Adaptive Integral Autoencoder (GAIA), an operator learning model that encodes the domain boundary and the interior field distribution into geometry tokens, and conditions integral transform layers on these tokens via cross-attention, allowing the kernel to adapt locally to geometric features. This yields a single architecture for forward (including BVPs) and inverse problems on arbitrary domains in one pass, without retraining, iterative optimization, or graph construction. We evaluate GAIA on seven 2D and 3D benchmarks, four of which are new or substantially extended benchmarks for inverse problems and BVP: electrical impedance tomography, optical tomography, 3D Darcy flow on varying geometries, and a modified setting of Poisson BVP on mechanical components benchmark (MCB). GAIA sets new state-of-the-art results on every inverse and BVP task, reducing median relative $L^2$ error by 64% on airfoil flow reconstruction and 27% on EIT relative to the next best amortized method, and outperforming all baselines on every shape category of MCB. On other forward problems, GAIA is competitive with specialized solvers while maintaining stable accuracy across point resolutions on which transformer-based baselines degrade.

AI.tldr: GAIA is a geometry-adaptive neural operator that uses tokens and cross-attention to handle forward/inverse PDE problems on arbitrary geometries in one pass, achieving SOTA results on boundary value and inverse problems.
AI.motivation: Existing geometry-adaptive neural operators are limited to forward problems where inputs/outputs share the same spatial domain, restricting their applicability to boundary value problems and inverse problems where inputs/outputs may be on different domains.
AI.method: GAIA encodes domain boundary and interior field distribution into geometry tokens, then conditions integral transform layers on these tokens via cross-attention, allowing kernels to adapt locally to geometric features for arbitrary domains.
AI.result: Sets new SOTA on all inverse and BVP tasks: 64% median error reduction on airfoil flow, 27% on EIT vs best amortized methods; outperforms all baselines on MCB; competitive with specialized solvers on forward problems while maintaining stable accuracy across resolutions.
AI.conclusion: GAIA provides a unified architecture for both forward and inverse PDE problems on arbitrary geometries without retraining or iterative optimization, significantly advancing operator learning for boundary value and inverse problems.

title: A Lightweight Self-Supervised Learning Framework for Multivariate Time Series using Hierarchical-JEPA on ECG Data
comment: 25 pages, 7 figures. Code will be made publicly available soon
summary: Data analysis in the medical domain often encounters scenarios involving a limited target dataset and a large, unannotated dataset with a general distribution. Under such circumstances, self-supervised learning (SSL) methods are highly effective for utilizing large datasets, making them a popular choice for electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis. This work presents the Event Reconstruction Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (ER-JEPA), a lightweight SSL framework for multivariate time series, whose name and two-fold hierarchical structure are inspired by the diagnostic approach of cardiologists. At its core, ER-JEPA features: (1) a two-stage structure that constructs representations for each time interval and subsequently processes these representations as a univariate time series, (2) the hierarchical integration of two Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs), and (3) a Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone. The structural concatenation of two JEPAs categorizes the model as a Hierarchical JEPA (H-JEPA), designed to encode multiple levels of abstract representations for enhanced prediction on complex tasks. This study reports a successful application of H-JEPA to 12-lead ECG data as a multivariate time series alongside an analysis of the sensitivity of hierarchical representation during the pretraining stage. Pretrained on approximately 180,000 10-second recordings, the model achieves state-of-the-art downstream performance on the ST-MEM benchmark, with rapid computation and minimal resource usage.

AI.tldr: ER-JEPA is a lightweight self-supervised learning framework for multivariate time series inspired by cardiologist diagnostic approaches, featuring hierarchical JEPA structure with ViT backbone that achieves state-of-the-art ECG analysis performance.
AI.motivation: Medical data analysis often deals with limited annotated target datasets alongside large unannotated general datasets. Self-supervised learning is effective for utilizing large datasets in ECG analysis, but needs frameworks that can capture hierarchical representations similar to how cardiologists analyze ECGs.
AI.method: ER-JEPA (Event Reconstruction Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture) features: 1) Two-stage structure building representations per time interval then processing as univariate series, 2) Hierarchical integration of two JEPAs forming H-JEPA, 3) Vision Transformer backbone. The two-level JEPA structure encodes multiple abstraction levels for complex tasks.
AI.result: Pretrained on ~180,000 10-second ECG recordings, ER-JEPA achieves state-of-the-art performance on ST-MEM benchmark with rapid computation and minimal resource usage. Also demonstrates sensitivity of hierarchical representation during pretraining.
AI.conclusion: ER-JEPA/H-JEPA successfully applies hierarchical SSL to 12-lead ECG analysis, providing an effective framework that mimics cardiologist diagnostic approaches while achieving strong performance with computational efficiency.

title: Efficient Compression of Structured and Unstructured Volumes via Learned 3D Gaussian Representation
summary: Recent work has shown that implicit neural representations (INRs) can be trained to effectively compress structured and unstructured volume data, allowing for direct data querying with a reduced memory footprint. However, as existing INRs for unstructured volumes do not encode geometry, they require partial mesh storage for later sampling, limiting achievable compression. At the same time, novel view synthesis methods have shown that explicit collections of 3D Gaussians can be used to accurately visualize volume data. In this work, we introduce an explicit model for volume data compression based on 3D Gaussian primitives. We reinterpret collections of 3D Gaussians as an explicit representation of a scalar field and use a sampling strategy that reconstructs scalar values at spatial locations through weighted aggregation of intersecting Gaussians. We develop optimized CUDA-accelerated pipelines for structured and unstructured model sampling, loss functions that encourage accurate domain encoding by our models, and a novel sampling-error based densification strategy. Our explicit formulation naturally encodes domain geometry, eliminating the need for mesh storage in unstructured volumes and introducing significantly higher compression opportunities. Compared to existing INRs, we demonstrate that our explicit model achieves competitive reconstruction quality with significant training speedups on structured volumes, while markedly outperforming in all metrics on unstructured volumes.

AI.tldr: 3D Gaussian primitives as explicit representation for volume data compression, encoding both geometry and scalar values, eliminating need for mesh storage in unstructured volumes
AI.motivation: Current implicit neural representations (INRs) for unstructured volumes don't encode geometry, requiring partial mesh storage for sampling and limiting compression potential. Need for better volume compression methods that can handle both structured and unstructured data efficiently.
AI.method: Use explicit 3D Gaussian primitives as representation of scalar field; weighted aggregation of intersecting Gaussians for reconstruction; optimized CUDA-accelerated sampling pipelines; novel sampling-error based densification strategy; geometry-encoding loss functions.
AI.result: Achieves competitive reconstruction quality with significant training speedups on structured volumes compared to INRs; markedly outperforms all metrics on unstructured volumes by eliminating mesh storage requirement.
AI.conclusion: Explicit 3D Gaussian model provides superior volume compression solution, especially for unstructured volumes where it encodes geometry naturally and enables much higher compression ratios than implicit representations.

title: Decision-Aware Training for Sample-Based Generative Models
summary: Sample-based generative models are increasingly used for probabilistic forecasting in high-stakes decision settings, yet their training objectives are blind to the decision maker's cost structure. These models are commonly trained with strictly proper scoring rules, such as the energy score, which allocate their training signal in proportion to data density, with no awareness of where forecast errors are most costly for downstream decisions. We therefore propose decision-aware training for sample-based generative models, augmenting the energy score objective with a differentiable decision loss that directly penalises the cost incurred by acting on the model's forecast. This combined loss is theoretically grounded, as the decision loss is itself a proper scoring rule. We validate our method on one synthetic and two real-world tasks, showing targeted improvements in cost-sensitive regions while retaining full probabilistic forecasts.

AI.tldr: Proposes decision-aware training for sample-based generative models by combining energy score with differentiable decision loss for better cost-sensitive probabilistic forecasting.
AI.motivation: Current sample-based generative models for probabilistic forecasting use strictly proper scoring rules (like energy score) that don't consider decision maker's cost structure, allocating training signal proportionally to data density regardless of where forecast errors are most costly for downstream decisions.
AI.method: Proposes decision-aware training by augmenting the energy score objective with a differentiable decision loss that directly penalizes the cost incurred by acting on the model's forecast. This combined loss maintains theoretical grounding since the decision loss is itself a proper scoring rule.
AI.result: Validated on one synthetic and two real-world tasks, showing targeted improvements in cost-sensitive regions while retaining full probabilistic forecasts.
AI.conclusion: Decision-aware training enables sample-based generative models to provide better probabilistic forecasts for cost-sensitive decision making by incorporating decision losses directly into training while maintaining theoretical properties.

title: QuasiMoTTo: Quasi-Monte Carlo Test-Time Scaling
summary: Scaling inference compute, by generating many parallel attempts per problem, is a costly but reliable lever for improving language model capabilities. By default these attempts are generated independently, wasting inference compute on redundant solutions. This waste seems unavoidable. After all, independence is what makes parallel sampling trivial to scale. However, this tradeoff is not fundamental: there is a rich design space of samplers that generate correlated but exact samples entirely in parallel. We explore this design space as an avenue for improving sample efficiency in scaling inference compute and reinforcement learning (RL). Concretely, we introduce QuasiMoTTo, which uses correlated samples as a drop-in replacement for i.i.d. samples. To generate these samples, QuasiMoTTo uses a reparameterization of autoregressive sampling as inverse-CDF sampling and draws the underlying uniforms with quasi-Monte Carlo (QMC); because QMC spreads the uniforms out more evenly than i.i.d., the resulting samples cover the output space with far less redundancy. Even though the batch is correlated, each sample is marginally distributed according to the language model, so we can use the batch for policy-gradient training. Our empirical analysis focuses on understanding how efficiently QuasiMoTTo can turn compute into performance. To evaluate correlated samplers, whose dependence breaks standard pass@k estimators, we first develop an unbiased bootstrap estimator. Across four reasoning benchmarks, QuasiMoTTo matches i.i.d. pass@k accuracy with 25-47% fewer samples. Strikingly, QuasiMoTTo often saturates an upper bound on pass@k that holds for any marginal-preserving sampler. We also apply QuasiMoTTo to policy-gradient RL (GRPO) where it matches i.i.d. performance with 50% fewer training steps. These gains come from higher coverage, which yields a stronger learning signal per batch.

AI.tldr: QuasiMoTTo improves sample efficiency by replacing i.i.d. samples with correlated quasi-Monte Carlo samples that cover output space more evenly, reducing redundancy while maintaining marginal distributions.
AI.motivation: Parallel sampling in language models wastes inference compute on redundant solutions. Independent sampling is easy to scale but inefficient, creating a need for correlated samplers that maintain exact marginal distributions while improving coverage.
AI.method: QuasiMoTTo reparameterizes autoregressive sampling as inverse-CDF sampling and uses quasi-Monte Carlo (QMC) to draw underlying uniforms, generating correlated samples that are more evenly distributed across output space than i.i.d. samples.
AI.result: Across four reasoning benchmarks, QuasiMoTTo matches i.i.d. pass@k accuracy with 25-47% fewer samples. In policy-gradient RL (GRPO), it matches i.i.d. performance with 50% fewer training steps by providing stronger learning signals through better coverage.
AI.conclusion: Correlated sampling via QuasiMoTTo offers significant sample efficiency improvements over independent sampling for both inference compute scaling and reinforcement learning, with the method often saturating theoretical upper bounds on performance for marginal-preserving samplers.

title: Neural Certificate Pricing for Combinatorial Optimization Problems
summary: Combinatorial optimization (CO) problems are difficult because certifiable discrete structure induces exponential search. One needs to search over the set exponentially many candidates to certify optimality, however, the structural feasibility of a path, packing, or cover can be verified in polynomial time once supplied. In this study, we introduce Neural Certificate Pricing (NCP) that exploits this asymmetry under an unsupervised learning framework. A neural network is trained to predict certificate-level dual prices, while a structured recovery layer constructs the induced primal marginal. NCP can be viewed as amortized separation: instead of enumerating violated inequalities, it learns the residual prices through which their aggregate effect enters recovery. When the certificate-consistency condition holds, the recovered marginal is globally feasible, and a local theory shows that first-order errors in the predicted price induce only second-order loss in objective value. Across three classes of CO problems, NCP either outperforms state-of-the-art neural baselines by large margins or matches them at a fraction of the computation time, and shows stronger out-of-distribution generalization.

AI.tldr: Neural Certificate Pricing (NCP) is an unsupervised learning method that predicts certificate-level dual prices for combinatorial optimization problems, enabling efficient solution recovery with strong generalization and reduced computation time.
AI.motivation: Combinatorial optimization problems are inherently difficult due to their discrete structure causing exponential search complexity. While verifying a feasible solution can be done in polynomial time, finding the optimal solution requires exploring exponentially many candidates. This asymmetry between verification and optimization presents an opportunity for machine learning to exploit.
AI.method: The paper introduces Neural Certificate Pricing (NCP), which uses an unsupervised learning framework where a neural network predicts certificate-level dual prices. A structured recovery layer then constructs the induced primal marginal from these predicted prices. NCP operates as "amortized separation" - instead of enumerating violated inequalities, it learns the residual prices through which their aggregate effect enters the recovery process.
AI.result: When the certificate-consistency condition holds, the recovered marginal is globally feasible. Theoretical analysis shows that first-order errors in the predicted price induce only second-order loss in objective value. Across three classes of CO problems, NCP either significantly outperforms state-of-the-art neural baselines or matches them while using a fraction of the computation time. The method also demonstrates stronger out-of-distribution generalization.
AI.conclusion: NCP effectively exploits the asymmetry between verification and optimization in combinatorial problems through a novel neural pricing approach. By learning to predict certificate-level dual prices in an unsupervised manner, it achieves superior performance and generalization while reducing computational burden compared to existing neural methods for combinatorial optimization.

title: Quantum vs. Classical Machine Learning: A Unified Empirical Comparison
comment: This paper has been accepted for a poster presentation at the 5th CCF Quantum Computation Conference (CQCC 2026) on August 3, 2026
summary: Quantum computing has emerged as a promising computational paradigm for machine learning (ML), with the potential to offer computational advantages over classical approaches. At this stage, the evidence supporting the performance and advantages of quantum machine learning (QML) models relative to classical models is insufficient.To address this gap, this paper presents an empirical study on the performance of QML models and their classical counterparts. We compare seven model pairs spanning supervised learning and reinforcement learning. Our results indicate that the evaluated quantum machine learning models do not yet surpass the classical baselines in overall prediction performance, policy stability, or training time. Nevertheless, QML remains a promising approach for filtering noise and controlling false positives. Our research findings summarize the challenges facing quantum machine learning across hardware environments, training efficiency, and convergence stability, providing a foundation for research into the robustness and parameter optimization of QML. This work is publicly available at https://github.com/Z-537-437/QML.

AI.tldr: Quantum machine learning models don't yet outperform classical baselines but show promise for noise filtering and false positive control.
AI.motivation: Current evidence supporting quantum ML advantages is insufficient, so empirical comparison of QML vs classical models is needed.
AI.method: Empirical study comparing 7 model pairs across supervised learning and reinforcement learning domains.
AI.result: QML models don't surpass classical baselines in prediction performance, policy stability, or training time, but can filter noise and control false positives effectively.
AI.conclusion: QML shows promise despite current limitations, and the research identifies challenges for future work in hardware, training efficiency, and convergence stability.

title: TiRex-2: Generalizing TiRex to Multivariate Data and Streaming
summary: We introduce TiRex-2, a recurrent xLSTM-based time series foundation model that generalizes the univariate TiRex to multivariate forecasting with both past and future covariates. Real-world forecasting is inherently sequential: observations arrive continuously, variables evolve jointly, and a subset of covariates is known ahead of time. Existing Transformer-based time series foundation models capture cross-variate dependencies but incur quadratic complexity in context length and require full-history recomputation as new observations arrive. TiRex-2 addresses these limitations through a memory-centric recurrent design that operates at constant per-patch cost under streaming. The model combines a bidirectional time mixer with an asymmetric grouped-attention variate mixer, enabling the integration of future-known covariates while preserving strict causality over target variables. To our knowledge, this is the first time series foundation model that achieves this combination of properties. To support scalable multivariate pretraining, we propose a synthetic coupling pipeline that composes diverse multivariate samples on the fly from large univariate corpora. Empirically, TiRex-2 achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on GIFT-Eval and fev-bench, remains stable when streamed to arbitrary context lengths, and maintains constant inference cost per patch. The model uses 38.4M active parameters in univariate mode, with an additional 44.1M parameters activated for multivariate forecasting.

AI.tldr: TiRex-2 is a recurrent xLSTM-based multivariate time series foundation model that enables efficient streaming forecasting with both past and future covariates while maintaining constant computational cost per patch.
AI.motivation: Real-world forecasting is inherently sequential with continuous observation arrival, joint variable evolution, and some covariates known ahead of time. Existing Transformer-based models have quadratic complexity and require full-history recomputation for new observations.
AI.method: Uses a memory-centric recurrent design with bidirectional time mixer and asymmetric grouped-attention variate mixer to integrate future covariates while preserving strict causality. Introduces synthetic coupling pipeline for scalable multivariate pretraining from univariate corpora.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on GIFT-Eval and fev-bench benchmarks, remains stable when streamed to arbitrary context lengths, and maintains constant inference cost per patch with 38.4M/44.1M parameters for univariate/multivariate modes.
AI.conclusion: TiRex-2 is the first time series foundation model combining streaming capability with future covariate integration and constant computational cost, addressing key limitations of Transformer-based approaches for real-world forecasting scenarios.

title: Is One Layer Enough? Training A Single Transformer Layer Can Match Full-Parameter RL Training
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a central component of post-training large language models (LLMs), yet little is understood about how RL adaptation is distributed across transformer layers. Existing approaches typically update all model parameters uniformly, implicitly assuming that every layer contributes similarly to the gains obtained during RL post-training. In this work, we challenge this assumption through a systematic layer-wise study of RL training. Surprisingly, we find that training a single transformer layer can recover most of the gains achieved by full-parameter RL training, and in some cases even surpass it. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the quantity layer contribution, which measures the fraction of full RL improvement recovered by training a layer in isolation. Across seven models spanning two model families (Qwen3, Qwen2.5), three RL algorithms (GRPO, GiGPO, Dr. GRPO), and multiple task domains including mathematical reasoning, code generation, and agentic decision-making, we observe a remarkably stable pattern: RL gains are highly concentrated in a small subset of, and in many cases even a single, transformer layers. More strikingly, the same structural pattern consistently emerges: high-contribution layers concentrate in the middle of the transformer stack, while layers near the input and output ends contribute substantially less. The resulting layer rankings remain strongly correlated across datasets, tasks, model families, and RL algorithms.

AI.tldr: RL gains in LLM post-training are highly concentrated in a small subset of transformer layers, often just a single layer, with the highest contributions consistently found in middle layers rather than input/output layers.
AI.motivation: Despite RL being central to LLM post-training, little is understood about how RL adaptation is distributed across transformer layers. Existing approaches update all parameters uniformly, assuming every layer contributes similarly to RL gains, but this assumption remains unverified.
AI.method: Conducted systematic layer-wise study of RL training across seven models (Qwen3, Qwen2.5 families), three RL algorithms (GRPO, GiGPO, Dr. GRPO), and multiple task domains. Introduced "layer contribution" metric to quantify fraction of full RL improvement recovered by training each layer in isolation.
AI.result: Surprisingly found that training a single transformer layer can recover most gains of full-parameter RL training, sometimes even surpassing it. RL gains are highly concentrated in small subset (often just one layer) of middle transformer layers, with remarkably stable patterns across models, tasks, domains, and algorithms.
AI.conclusion: RL adaptation in LLMs is not uniformly distributed but highly concentrated in specific middle layers, suggesting current full-parameter approaches may be inefficient. This stable structural pattern across diverse conditions indicates fundamental properties of how RL affects transformer architecture.
